headline: Coffee Market Brief — 8 April 2026
key_takeaways: The latest Sucafina report says the market recently finished around 286 USc/lb.
The board has broken below roughly 290 USc/lb support, which keeps short-term downside pressure in play.
The report points to a possible near-term test around $2.75/lb.

news_drivers.text: Kitchen scraps like coffee grounds can be composted to create nutrient-rich soil for gardens.
Africa earns less than 10% of the global chocolate market despite producing 70% of the world's cocoa.
India's coffee output is estimated at 4.03 lakh tonnes for the 2025-26 coffee year, potentially impacting exports.

risks: Geopolitical headlines could trigger broad commodity volatility and fast intraday swings in coffee.
Macro risk-off flows may drive coffee lower even if coffee-specific fundamentals do not worsen.
A break of support can attract technical selling and extend downside before value buyers step in.
Export or logistics disruptions can lift replacement costs even if futures soften.
roaster_actions: Avoid fixing large volumes all at once; scale coverage in tranches while the board is probing lower.
Separate flat-price decisions from differential decisions, because physical replacement costs can stay firm even when futures weaken.
Set buy triggers around key technical levels so you can respond quickly if the market flushes lower or rebounds sharply.
Use the current front-month snapshot of 321.35 with your own margin targets before extending forward cover.
raw_report: This past week, coffee futures basis the most active May contract fell 12.35 cents from Tuesday to Tuesday to end at 286 US cents per pound. Most of this price action took place today at the time of writing. I expected last week for the market to reestablish a range of $2.90 to $3.00/lb for the week and this is what broadly occurred as geopolitically based speculative inflows balanced against increased selling interest from origin. Today's price action is a bit dramatic and began with a general risk-off environment following the US President's escalatory and inflammatory notes regarding the ongoing conflict with Iran. A more prolonged war can result in a broader commodity sell-off due to inflationary pressures caused by higher energy prices, ultimately impacting consumer spending. From the initial wave down, we broke below a critical 290 USc/lb support level, which indicates more selling from momentum funds nearby. As we look toward next week, most of the risk hinges on the Iran conflict, with further escalation being negative for prices. In other commodity news, Cocoa prices, which have been recently compared to coffee, have fallen to 136 US cents/lb, showing how brutal Bear cycles can become once supply concerns ease. My estimate for this week is for the market to try to test resistance at $2.75/lb.
